text.title: confidence-thresholded-cascade Worked Examples
text.summary: Three production case studies: customer-support classifier, FAQ chatbot, code-review triage.

example.title: 10K tickets/day, Haiku → Sonnet at 0.88
example.p: Baseline (Sonnet only): $40/day. Cascade: 78% of tickets handled by Haiku at $0.0005 each, 22% escalated to Sonnet at $0.01 each. Total: $11/day, ~75% saved. Monthly eval showed zero quality regression. Threshold 0.88 came from calibration on 200 historical tickets where Haiku at 0.88+ was 96% accurate.

example.title: 12K Q/day, Haiku-only acceptance at 0.85
example.p: Most questions are repeats with strong priors. Calibration showed Haiku at 0.85+ matched human-rated quality 91% of the time. After deployment, 91% of traffic stayed with Haiku at $0.0005, the remaining 9% escalated to Sonnet at $0.005. Total: $5/day vs $90 single-Sonnet, ~94% saved.

example.title: Trivial vs deep review split
example.p: Cheap model decides "trivial PR" vs "needs review". 60% of PRs are dependency bumps and trivial fixes — handled by Haiku with confidence >0.90 (calibrated at 98% accuracy). The 40% that aren't get escalated to Opus for deep review. Cost: $0.0035 weighted average vs $0.05 always-Opus → 93% saved on review traffic.

example.title: Blog-post generation with cascade
example.p: A team tried cascade on blog-post drafts. "Confidence" was meaningless for creative output — Haiku confidently produced low-quality drafts (high `confidence_0_to_1`), so nothing escalated. Result: cascade made quality worse than single-Sonnet. Lesson: cascade fits classification and extraction, not generative writing.

example.title: Escalation-rate spike after prompt change
example.p: Escalation rate stable at 22% for months. After a prompt update, it jumped to 67%. Root cause: the new prompt confused Haiku, which started rating its own confidence lower. Reverted prompt; rate normalized to 22% within one day. This is why telemetry on escalation rate matters — it was the leading indicator before any quality complaint hit.
